# OpenStax, Business Ethics (deep reference)

**Source:** OpenStax (2018). *Business Ethics*. Houston, TX: Rice University. Senior contributing authors Stephen M. Byars (USC Marshall School of Business) and Kurt Stanberry (University of Houston–Downtown); contributing authors David Shapiro (Pennsylvania State University), Barbara Boerner (Brevard College), Robert Brancatelli (Fordham University), Wade Chumney (California State University, Northridge), Laura Dendinger (Wayne State College), Bill Nantz (Houston Community College), Mark Poepsel (Southern Illinois University Edwardsville). ISBNs: 978-1-947172-56-2 (color paperback); 978-1-59399-577-5 (B&W paperback); 978-1-947172-57-9 (digital). Original publication year 2018. Licence: **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)**. URL: https://openstax.org/details/books/business-ethics.
**Scope:** open-nc
**Structure:** Preface; eleven substantive chapters covering ethics foundations and ethical theories (Aristotelian and Confucian virtue, utilitarianism, deontology, justice theory); stakeholder definition and prioritisation; corporate social responsibility and three special stakeholders (society, environment, government); the impact of culture and time on business ethics; what employers owe employees; what employees owe employers; recognising the rights of all (diversity, accommodation, sexual identification, income inequality, animal rights); professions under the microscope (entrepreneurship, advertising, insurance, health care); changing work environments and future trends; an epilogue on becoming an ethical professional. Three substantive appendices follow: A (lives of ethical philosophers — Aristotle, Bentham, Mill, Kant, Rawls), B (profiles of ten contemporary ethical business leaders), C (a succinct theory of business ethics by the lead author).
**Citation style:** `(Ch N, "Section name")` for chapters; `(App {A|B|C}, "Section name")` for appendices. The PDF was converted with pymupdf4llm 0.2.9, which does not preserve PDF page anchors; section names from the converted markdown are used in their place.
**Coverage:** substantive content (chapter introductions and numbered sections plus all three substantive appendices); excluded scaffolding sections per chapter: **Key Terms**, **Summary**, **Assessment Questions**, **Endnotes**. Back-matter Answer Key and Index are excluded. Coverage is partial in the sense that scaffolding/pedagogical apparatus is omitted; all substantive exposition is included.

## Author's thesis (paraphrased from source)

The book frames business ethics as the practice by which "companies and their agents abide by the law and respect the rights of their stakeholders, particularly their customers, clients, employees, and the surrounding community and environment" [V] (Ch 1.1, "Acting with Integrity"). The text's central conviction is that ethical business is not in tension with profitability across the long run; the most-admired companies tend to be among the most profitable, and ethical conduct generates *goodwill* — reputation, brand value, employee commitment, and customer loyalty — which in turn supports profit (Ch 1.2, "Stockholders, Stakeholders, and Goodwill"). The opposing view — Milton Friedman's claim that the only "social responsibility of a business is to increase its profits" [V] (Ch 1.2, "Profitability and Success: Thinking Long Term", citing Friedman 1970) — is acknowledged as historically influential but treated as inadequate to the realities of contemporary business and the social contract a corporation enters into when society grants it the right to incorporate (Ch 1.2, Ch 4.1).

A second load-bearing claim is that *a single, universally applied ethical standard* is rationally superior to ethical relativism [AR] (Ch 1.3, "Multiple versus Single Ethical Standards"). Whether one reasons from utilitarianism, deontology, or virtue ethics, the rational conclusion is that one should "abide by a single ethical code for all interactions with all persons in all contexts" [AP] (Ch 1.3). The book uses this stance to reject the "when in Rome" defence of bribery, kickbacks, sweatshop conditions, and corruption in international business (Ch 5, Ch 7.4).

A third load-bearing claim is that *all stakeholders matter*, not just shareholders, and that business managers must develop the discipline of identifying, categorising, and prioritising stakeholder claims as a core managerial competence [AR] (Ch 3, especially 3.1-3.3). The book traces a century-long legal evolution from strict shareholder primacy in *Dodge v. Ford Motor Company* (1919) toward greater management latitude under the business-judgment rule in *Shlensky v. Wrigley* (1968) and *Burwell v. Hobby Lobby* (2014) (Ch 4.1, "Balancing the Many Responsibilities of a Corporation").

A fourth load-bearing claim is that the *moral minimum* — legal compliance — is rarely sufficient; ethical conduct requires going *above and beyond* the law to honour stakeholder interests, especially where the law lags behind ethical reflection [AR] (Ch 1.1, Ch 3.1, Ch 4.1, Ch 6.1). Concepts of *ethical minimum* (the least a company can do to claim ethical positioning) and *ethical maximum* (the strongest action available) are introduced as a tool for managers and stakeholders to evaluate corporate conduct [V] (Ch 3.1, "Ethical Responsibilities Often Extend Beyond Legal Requirements"). The text repeatedly takes the position that ethical maximums protect long-term profitability and reputation more reliably than ethical minimums.

A fifth load-bearing claim is that *the formation of conscience* matters more than compliance training; ethics is "a way of life, not a hurdle to overcome" [V] (Ch 11.3, "The Role of Ethical Top Leadership"). The book repeatedly argues that organisations succumb to ethics crises when they neglect organisational culture and individual character formation, however sophisticated their compliance apparatus (Ch 5.4, Ch 11). Ethical leadership at the top — the *tone at the top* — is presented as the most effective means by which ethical behaviour is learned in a company.

## Part I: Foundations — what business ethics is and why it matters (Ch 1)

### Ethics, integrity, and stakeholders

The text defines *ethics* as "the standards of behavior to which we hold ourselves in our personal and professional lives" [V] (Ch 1, "Introduction") and *integrity* as "unity between what we say and what we do" — adherence to a code of ethics implying trustworthiness and incorruptibility [V] (Ch 1.1, "Acting with Integrity"). *Stakeholders* are individuals and entities affected by the business's decisions: "clients, customers, suppliers, investors, retailers, employees, the media, the government, members of the surrounding community, competitors, and even the environment" [AP] (Ch 1.1, "Acting with Integrity"). Business ethics is the conduct by which "companies and their agents abide by the law and respect the rights of their stakeholders" [V] (Ch 1.1).

The text distinguishes ethical responsibility from legal *compliance* — "the extent to which a company conducts its business operations in accordance with applicable regulations, statutes, and laws" [V] (Ch 1.1, "Acting with Integrity"). Compliance is a baseline minimum: "Behaving ethically requires that we meet the mandatory standards of the law, but that is not enough. For example, an action may be legal that we personally consider unacceptable" [V] (Ch 1.1).

### Three normative ethical theories

The book introduces three normative families of ethical theory that recur throughout: examining *ends* (utilitarianism and consequentialism — the greatest good for the greatest number), examining *means* (deontology — actions are evaluated by adherence to duty regardless of consequence; Kant is the named figure), and examining *character* (virtue theory — Greek and Confucian sources, focusing on habituated character formed through practice) [AP] (Ch 1.1, "Ends, Means, and Character in Business").

*Phronesis* — practical wisdom — is introduced from Aristotle as a kind of tacit knowledge "acquired from experience that enables people to make prudent judgments and take actions based on the actual situation, guided by values and morals" [V] (Ch 1.1, "Aristotle and the Concept of Phronesis", citing Nonaka and Takeuchi). Phronetic leaders practice moral discernment, quickly assess situations, create shared sense of purpose, communicate via metaphor and story, and encourage practical wisdom in others (Ch 1.1).

### Stockholders, stakeholders, and goodwill

*Stockholders* (or shareholders) are "individuals and institutions that own stock (or shares) in a corporation" [AP] (Ch 1.2, "Stockholders, Stakeholders, and Goodwill"). The book notes that "considering only stockholders and short-term impacts on them is one of the most common errors business managers make" [V] (Ch 1.2). It recommends a three-column stakeholder spreadsheet: (1) stakeholders in priority order, (2) interests and goals, (3) likely impact of the decision (Ch 1.2).

*Goodwill* is "the positive feeling stakeholders have for any particular company" — including reputation, brand value, intellectual capital and workforce attitude, and customer loyalty (Ch 1.2). Goodwill cannot be earned in a short time; it is generated by ethical behaviour that has positive influence on each component. *Corporate culture* is the "shared beliefs, values, and behaviors that create the internal or organizational context within which managers and employees interact" [V] (Ch 1.2). Practising ethical behaviour at all levels cultivates an ethical corporate culture.

### Short-term versus long-term perspectives; corporate social responsibility

A *short-term perspective* focuses intensely on quarterly profit; a *long-term perspective* "is a more balanced view of profit maximization that recognizes that the impacts of a business decision may not manifest for a longer time" [V] (Ch 1.2, "Profitability and Success: Thinking Long Term"). The Toyota Way is given as an example: Toyota "was content to sell its cars at a slight loss" in the U.S. for years, prioritising long-term trust and reputation over short-term profit [AE] (Ch 1.2).

The text frames the corporation as entering a *social contract* with society: in return for the right to incorporate, "a company gives back to the society that permits it to exist, benefiting the community at the same time it enriches itself" (Ch 1.2). This grounds the introduction of *corporate social responsibility (CSR)* — "the practice by which a business views itself within a broader context, as a member of society with certain implicit social obligations and environmental responsibilities" [V] (Ch 1.2, "A Brief Introduction to Corporate Social Responsibility").

### Single ethical standard; ethical relativism rejected

*Ethical relativism* is the position that "there is no right or wrong and what is ethical depends solely on the context" [V] (Ch 1.3, "Multiple versus Single Ethical Standards"). The book argues against ethical relativism and for a single coherent ethical standard applicable to family, work, religious community, and strangers alike. The argument runs that proponents of all three normative theories converge on the conclusion that a rational ethical actor should adopt a single standard:

> "A deontologist would argue that you should adhere to particular duties in performing your actions, regardless of the parties with whom you interact. A utilitarian would say that any act you take should result in the greatest good for the greatest number. A virtue ethicist would state that you cannot be virtuous if you lack integrity in your behavior toward all."
>
> [V] (Ch 1.3)

The text notes that universal principles within a single code typically include "honesty, integrity, loyalty, fairness, respect for law, and respect for others" [V] (Ch 1.3).

## Part II: Ethical theory — antiquity to the present (Ch 2)

### Aristotelian virtue ethics

Aristotle's central ethical concept is *eudaimonia* — happiness in the sense of human flourishing, attained through the exercise of reason. Aristotle wrote: "But what is happiness? If we consider what the function of man is, we find that happiness is a virtuous activity of the soul." [V] (Ch 2.1, "Aristotelian Virtue Ethics"). And: "Happiness is a kind of activity; and an activity clearly is developed and is not a piece of property already in one's possession." [V] (Ch 2.1, citing *Nicomachean Ethics*).

Aristotle distinguished two virtue families: *intellectual* virtues (knowledge, wisdom, prudence) acquired through learning; and *moral* virtues (courage, self-control, liberality, magnificence, honor, patience, amiability) acquired through habituation [AP] (Ch 2.1). *Phronesis* (prudence, practical wisdom) is the central intellectual virtue, helping individuals "avoid excess and deficiency and arrive at the **golden mean** between the two" [V] (Ch 2.1). The *magnanimous individual* is the paragon — self-knowing, not rash or submissive, controlled, prudent. Aristotle: "magnanimity seems to be a sort of crown of the virtues, because it enhances them and is never found apart from them. This makes it hard to be truly magnanimous, because it is impossible without all-round excellence." [V] (Ch 2.1).

Aristotle held that *action precedes character*: "courage resulted from incremental change, small steps taken over time that molded the person's character" [AP] (Ch 2.1, "Aristotelian Virtue Ethics"). Honour was both an individual characteristic and a function of group membership. Aristotle was suspicious of business and warned that wealth "is obviously not the good that we are seeking, because it serves only as a means; i.e., for getting something else" [V] (Ch 2.1, "Honorable Behavior in Business"). What concerned Aristotle was greed — an "excess that tipped the scales of justice" — not money making per se. The text introduces three forms of justice from Athenian thought: *legal* (city-state's responsibility to establish fair laws), *commutative* (between individuals, correcting harms), *distributive* (city-state's duty to distribute benefits and burdens equitably) [AP] (Ch 2.1, "Three Forms of Justice").

### Confucian virtue ethics

Confucius (551–479 BCE) addressed the social disintegration of China's "Period of the Warring States". He called for a "return to **li**", the proper order of the universe expressed through ritual acts (Ch 2.2, "Social Upheaval in Ancient China"). The Confucian goal was to form a *junzi*: "a person who was gracious, magnanimous, and cultured: in other words, a flourishing human being" [V] (Ch 2.2, "Confucian Virtue Ethics").

The keystone is the *dao of humanity* — the Way. Confucius identified three means: "whole-hearted sincerity and truthfulness," the "constant mean," and "expediency" (*quan*) [V] (Ch 2.2). Sincerity meant loyalty to truth, not blind loyalty to a ruler. The constant mean meant balance between excess and deficiency, expressed in the five great relationships: parent/child, husband/wife, elder/junior sibling, master/apprentice, ruler/subject. *Quan* — originally a piece of metal balancing scales — is applied "when weighing options in a moral dilemma and acts as a counterbalance to achieve fairness, enabling parties in a transaction to arrive at an equitable agreement" [V] (Ch 2.2, "Confucian Virtue Ethics").

The text quotes *Analects* on the ethical professional: "A gentleman will not, for the space of a meal, depart from humanity. In haste and flurry, he adheres to it; in fall and stumble, he adheres by it." [V] (Ch 2.3, "Personal and Professional Roles").

### Comparing East and West

Both Aristotelian and Confucian systems share the theme of *control* — through deliberative phronesis (West) or self-regulation (East) [AP] (Ch 2.3, "East Meets West"). Both prize the integration of personal and professional life: the "modern tendency to compartmentalize various aspects of ourselves to accommodate circumstances would have puzzled those living in ancient Greece or China" [V] (Ch 2.3, "Personal and Professional Roles"). The locus differs: Aristotle places ethics on the individual, Confucius on the family.

### Utilitarianism: Bentham and Mill

Jeremy Bentham (1748–1842) sought to make ethics empirical, quantifiable, and reproducible. His *fundamental axiom* was that "all social morals and government legislation should aim for producing the greatest happiness for the greatest number of people" [AP] (Ch 2.4, "Maximizing Utility"). Utilitarianism's characteristics: "(1) universality, because it applies to all acts of human behavior, even those that appear to be done from altruistic motives; (2) objectivity, meaning it operates beyond individual thought, desire, and perspective; (3) rationality, because it is not based in metaphysics or theology; and (4) quantifiability in its reliance on utility." [V] (Ch 2.4). Bentham's *hedonic calculus* measured proposed actions on intensity, duration, certainty, and probability of consequence (Ch 2.4). Utilitarianism is a *consequentialist* theory — actions are judged solely by their consequences, "without regard to character, motivation, or any understanding of good and evil" [V] (Ch 2.4).

John Stuart Mill (1806–1873) refined Bentham by adding the *harm principle*: the "only purpose for which power can be rightfully exercised over any member of a civilized community, against his will, is to prevent harm to others. His own good, either physical or moral, is not a sufficient warrant." [V] (Ch 2.4, "Synthesizing Rights and Utility"). Mill saw free speech as the "foundational right, reflective of human nature, from which all others rights derive their meaning" [AP] (Ch 2.4, "The Role of Utilitarianism in Contemporary Business").

### Deontology: Kant

Immanuel Kant (1724–1804) focused on motives rather than consequences. The *categorical imperative*: "Act only according to that maxim whereby you can, at the same time, will that it should become a universal law." [V] (Ch 2.5, "Aroused From 'Dogmatic Slumber'"). Kant's two suppositions: act on the basis of goodwill rather than self-interest at others' expense; never treat others as means toward ends benefiting ourselves "without consideration of them also as ends in themselves" [AP] (Ch 2.5).

Deontology, derived from Greek *deon* (duty), is non-consequentialist and non-situationalist. The book's appendix on Kant restates the categorical imperative in two expressions: "a moral agent ... is free to act only in ways that he or she would permit any other moral agent to act"; and "we must treat others as ends in themselves and not just as means to our own ends" [AP] (App A, "The Ethics of Kant: Deontology"). Kant: "the moral law is an imperative, which commands categorically, because the law is unconditioned." [V] (Ch 2.5, "Kantian Business Ethics").

### Justice theory: Rawls

John Rawls (1921–2002) presented *justice as fairness* in *A Theory of Justice* (1971). Rawls's three principles: (1) *original position* (a hypothetical pre-political situation), (2) *veil of ignorance* (people imagine themselves with no identity-specifying attributes), (3) *unanimity of acceptance* (all must agree to the contract) [AP] (Ch 2.6, "Justice Theory"). The five procedural steps: enter the contract, agree unanimously, include basic conditions like freedom of speech, "maximizing the welfare of the most disadvantaged persons", and ensure stability (Ch 2.6).

The text presents the cake-division illustration: "the best way to divide the cake is to have the person slicing the cake take the last piece" [AP] (Ch 2.6). This illustrates "pure procedural justice".

The book frames Rawls's distinctive advantage as a *method* rather than content: "it can be applied in a variety of ways and in multiple disciplines, because it can be adapted to just about any value-laden content" [V] (Ch 2.6, "The Problem of Redistribution"). The opposing view from Robert Nozick is registered: the state may not "ethically be used to deprive someone of property he or she has legally obtained or inherited in order to distribute it to others who are in need of it" [AP] (Ch 2.6).

## Part III: Stakeholders — definition and prioritisation (Ch 3)

### Stakeholder relationships and ethical extension beyond the law

Stakeholders include "customers, clients, employees, shareholders, communities, the environment, the government, and the media (traditional and social), among others" [V] (Ch 3.1, "Stakeholder Relationships"). All stakeholders matter, "but not all have equal priority". Internal stakeholders — board, CEO, executives, employees — are distinguished from external — customers, suppliers, governments, media, communities (Ch 3.1).

The book introduces the foundational distinction between *ethical maximum* — "the strongest action a company can choose to behave ethically in a given situation" [V] (Ch 3.1, "Ethical Responsibilities Often Extend Beyond Legal Requirements") — and *ethical minimum* — "the least a company might do to claim it holds an ethically positive position". The Samsung washer recall is given as ethical-maximum example: Samsung issued full refunds to all owners of the affected model regardless of whether they had complained, plus free pickup, covering nearly three million machines (Ch 3.1).

### Three theoretical approaches to stakeholder claims (Donaldson and Preston)

The text adopts the Donaldson and Preston taxonomy: *descriptive*, *instrumental*, *normative* [BT] (Ch 3.2, "The Ethical Basis of Stakeholders' Claims"). Descriptive: "the company as composed of various stakeholder groups, each with its own interests" — develop the most accurate model and balance interests fairly [V] (Ch 3.2). Instrumental: connects stakeholder management to financial outcomes — proper management contributes to a positive bottom line. Normative: "stakeholders as ends in themselves rather than simply as means to achieve better financial results" — "the interests of all stakeholders are of intrinsic value" [V] (Ch 3.2). Donaldson and Preston argue that the fundamental basis of stakeholder theory is *normative*; the book endorses this position.

### Stakeholder categorisation (Grunig and Hunt linkage model)

Four stakeholder categories from the Grunig-Hunt organisational linkage model [BT] (Ch 3.2, "Defining Stakeholder Categories"):

- **Enabling stakeholders** — stockholders, legislatures, government regulators, boards of directors. "They permit the firm to function" [V] (Ch 3.2).
- **Normative stakeholders** — competitors and peers. They "influence the norms or informal rules of the industry" [V] (Ch 3.2).
- **Functional stakeholders** — those who influence inputs (suppliers, employees, unions) and outputs (customers, distributors, retailers).
- **Diffused stakeholders** — NGOs, voters, mass media. "Less direct relationships but potential for meaningful impacts on firms" [V] (Ch 3.2).

The same authors distinguish four *publics* by awareness and capacity: nonpublic (no problem), latent (problem unrecognised), aware (problem recognised), active (organised in response) [BT] (Ch 3.2, "The Chicago Tylenol Murders"). The Tylenol case is presented as Johnson & Johnson modelling enlightened communication across publics; Volkswagen's emissions cover-up as the failure mode (Ch 3.2).

### Stakeholder prioritisation

*Stakeholder prioritisation* is "the process of deciding which stakeholders to focus on and in what sequence" [V] (Ch 3.3, "Key Terms"; Ch 3.3, "Stakeholder Prioritization"). The text introduces a power × interest matrix [AE] (Ch 3.3, Figure 3.5): high-power-high-interest = key stakeholder; if a problem arises, priority rises. *Exigency* (urgency of a stakeholder claim) combines with inherent priority to drive resource allocation.

The book references the MITRE five-step stakeholder-management system: identify stakeholders (and reidentify periodically), gather data on concerns, analyse, present results to decision makers, inform stakeholders that their concerns were considered [BT] (Ch 3.3, "Managing Stakeholder Expectations"). Lindahl is cited on nonprofits: philanthropies have an ethical "stewardship" obligation to safeguard donations (Ch 3.3).

### Corporate social responsibility and the triple bottom line

The book reintroduces *CSR* as a stakeholder-oriented philosophy in which a company "views itself within a broader context" (Ch 3.4). The *triple bottom line (TBL)* — described by John Elkington in 1994 — measures three dimensions: economic, social, environmental — "people, planet, profit" [V] (Ch 3.4, "People, Planet, Profit: The Triple Bottom Line"). Externalities — social and environmental impacts — must be taken into account.

*Greenwashing* is "carrying out superficial CSR efforts that merely cover up systemic ethics problems for the sake of public relations" [V] (Ch 3.4, "Key Terms"). Coca-Cola's CSR statements about stakeholder engagement are juxtaposed with the company's water footprint (a Chiapas, Mexico bottling plant consumes more than one billion liters of water daily; only about half the local population has running water) [V] (Ch 3.4, "CSR as Public Relations Tool"). Ben and Jerry's reliance on confined-animal feeding operation (CAFO) suppliers under Unilever ownership is offered as another candidate for greenwashing critique (Ch 3.4).

## Part IV: Three special stakeholders — society, environment, government (Ch 4)

### Corporate law and the shareholder-primacy debate

*Limited liability* shields shareholders, directors, and managers from corporate debt liability beyond their share investment (Ch 4.1, "The Advantages of Corporate Status"). Society enables corporations to exist via incorporation laws, and "a reasonable person could conclude that corporations owe a debt to society in return" — the *quid pro quo* of CSR (Ch 4.1).

The book traces a century-long judicial debate over *shareholder primacy* (Ch 4.1, "Balancing the Many Responsibilities of a Corporation"): *Dodge v. Ford Motor Company* (1919, Michigan Supreme Court) ruled that Ford "must operate the Ford Motor Company primarily in the profit-maximizing interests of its shareholders" [V] [BT] (Ch 4.1). The same case upheld the *business judgment rule* — officers and directors are not liable for losses incurred when decisions were "reasonable and made in good faith" [V] (Ch 4.1). *Shlensky v. Wrigley* (1968) gave the Wrigley Company board significant discretion to consider community interests over short-term shareholder returns [BT] (Ch 4.1). *Burwell v. Hobby Lobby* (2014) extended this latitude (5–4 decision): "corporate law does not require for-profit corporations to pursue profit at the expense of everything else" [V] (Ch 4.1, citing the majority opinion). Justice Alito wrote: "A corporation is simply a form of organization used by human beings to achieve desired ends." [V] (Ch 4.1).

The text contrasts Stephen Bainbridge ("If directors were allowed to deviate from shareholder wealth maximization, they would inevitably turn to indeterminate balancing standards, which provide no accountability" [V] [BT]) with Lynn Stout ("Modern corporate law does not require for-profit corporations to pursue profit at the expense of everything else, and many do not" [V] [BT]) (Ch 4.1, "The Two Sides of the Corporate Responsibility Debate").

The *moral minimum* is introduced: "the minimal actions or practices a business must undertake to satisfy the base threshold for acting ethically" [V] (Ch 4, "Key Terms"). Honouring obligations to all stakeholders, "including those who own no stock shares — is the moral minimum a firm must undertake to satisfy the base threshold for acting ethically" [V] (Ch 4.1).

### Sustainability, environmental justice, and the rights of nature

*Corporate personhood* — the legal doctrine that a corporation has some of the same legal rights and responsibilities as natural persons — was extended to free-speech rights for corporations in *Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission* (2010) [BT] (Ch 4.2, "Environmental Justice"). The text raises the question whether the environment should enjoy similar legal status: should environmental advocates have been able to sue BP "on behalf of the entire Gulf of Mexico for harm created by the 2010 Deepwater Horizon oil spill" [V]? Ecuador rewrote its Constitution in 2008 to officially recognise nature's rights (Ch 4.2). *Earth jurisprudence* (Cullinan, Berry) is "an interpretation of law and governance based on the belief that society will be sustainable only if we recognize the legal rights of Earth as if it were a person" [AP] (Ch 4.2).

The *tragedy of the commons* (Lloyd, Hardin) is the human tendency to "use as much of a free resource as they want, without regard for the needs of others or for the long-term environmental effects" [V] (Ch 4.2, "Environmental Justice").

*Sustainability* is "a long-term approach to the interaction between business activity and societal impact on the environment and other stakeholders" [V] (Ch 4, "Key Terms"). The book argues sustainability is good business: McKinsey's 2010 survey found 76 per cent of executives agreed sustainability provides shareholders long-term value; the 2014 survey found "Sustainability's Strategic Worth" — over one-third of companies cited cost cutting, improved operations, and efficiency as primary sustainability motivators (Ch 4.2). Grant Thornton's 2014 report (over 2,500 interviews in approximately 35 countries) found cost management as the key reason for sustainability (67%) [BT] (Ch 4.2).

The *Global 100* (Corporate Knights) lists the world's most sustainable companies; 2018 placed Cisco seventh, Siemens ninth (Ch 4.2). $250 invested in Global 100 companies in 2005 grew to $580 by 2015 versus $520 for a typical index fund — "about 25 percent higher" cumulative return on high-sustainability firms [V] (Ch 4.2).

The *carbon footprint* is "the amount of carbon dioxide and other carbon compounds released by the consumption of fossil fuels" [V] (Ch 4, "Key Terms"). *Cap and trade* limits emissions and allows companies to trade pollution allowances (Ch 4.2, "The High Cost of Inaction"). A *carbon tax* — a "pay-to-pollute" system — is proposed by the Climate Leadership Council; supporters include Exxon Mobil, Shell, BP, Total. Senator Sheldon Whitehouse: "You levy a price on a thing you don't want—carbon pollution—and you use the revenue to help with things you do want." A US carbon tax of $45 per metric ton would reduce emissions by more than 40% in the first decade (Ch 4.2). Forty countries plus all EU members, New Zealand, and Japan have already enacted some form of programme (Ch 4.2). A *Pigovian tax* (named after A.C. Pigou) imposes a fee on activities that cause "negative externalities" on third parties (Ch 4.2).

### Government and the private sector

The text addresses public-private partnerships (P3s) and the "revolving door" between government regulation and the private sector (Ch 4.3, "The Revolving Door between Government Regulation and the Private Sector"). Goldman Sachs alumni in senior government positions (US Treasury Secretary Mnuchin; ECB President Mario Draghi; Bank of England Governor Mark Carney) are cited [AE] (Ch 4.3). A 2008 GAO survey found nearly 90,000 former Defense Department employees subsequently employed by private-sector defense contractors over the prior eight years (Ch 4.3). Federal post-employment restrictions: a one-year ban on appearance before former agency for senior officials; two years for "very senior" officials; penalties up to $50,000 per violation for individuals and $500,000 for companies; criminal penalties up to five years (Ch 4.3).

Three positions on government regulation are sketched: a *states' rights* position (Tenth Amendment, narrow Commerce Clause), a libertarian "less is better" position, and a federalist position attributed to Hamilton in the *Federalist Papers* (Ch 4.3, "Government Regulation and the Constitution"). The text takes no consensus position but warns against weak regulation that lets businesses "cut corners".

The *BP Deepwater Horizon* disaster is examined: 11 workers killed, 17 injured, 5 million barrels of oil spilled (Ch 4.3, "BP Deepwater Horizon Oil Spill and Government Regulation"). Transocean did not install an "acoustically triggered shutoff valve" because Congress had not mandated it, "largely as a result of oil industry lobbying" (Ch 4.3). Norway and Brazil require these devices; "Two-thirds of Statoil, its largest oil company, is owned by the government, and, as a result, the company does not lobby the government for weakened regulation" [V] (Ch 4.3).

*Citizens United v. FEC* (2010, 5–4) ruled that "laws preventing corporations from using general treasury funds for political advertising violated the First Amendment's guarantee of freedom of speech" [AP] (Ch 4.3). *Sarbanes-Oxley* (SOX, 2002), passed in response to Enron and WorldCom, mandates reporting transparency, makes CEO/CFO personally responsible for audit errors, prohibits company loans to executives, and protects whistleblowers (Ch 4.3).

The Vopak settlement (Ch 4.3) is given as an example of state-federal cooperation: Vopak agreed to install state-of-the-art pollution controls, deploy infrared cameras for fugitive emissions, and pay a $2.5 million civil penalty (Ch 4.3).

## Part V: Culture and time — the influence of geography, history, and religion (Ch 5)

### Enculturation, acculturation, and the cultural shaping of business

*Enculturation* is "the process by which humans learn the rules, customs, skills, and values to participate in a society" [V] (Ch 5, "Key Terms"). *Acculturation* is "the cultural transmission and socialization process that stems from cultural exchange" [V] (Ch 5, "Key Terms"). The 1626 "purchase" of Manhattan is presented as a cross-cultural transaction with mismatched assumptions about property [AE] (Ch 5.1, "Business Encounters Culture").

The book argues that "business is not culturally neutral" — its dominant mode "displays a mindset that is Western and primarily English-speaking" emphasising individualism and competition [V] (Ch 5.1, "Balancing Beliefs"). However, modern global business must balance profitability with responsibility to public interest and planet. Three kinds of company belief are catalogued: about purpose of business, about mission, and about itself (the company's enculturated self-understanding) (Ch 5.1).

The Petrobras Operation Car Wash scandal (Brazil, 2014) is offered as a case of corruption: executives received bribes and kickbacks ("upward of five percent of the contract amount"), funnelled into slush funds for political campaigns and luxury items (Ch 5.1). Siemens paid billions in fines for bribing government officials in multiple countries (Ch 5.1).

### Business ethics over time

The book traces ethical evolution through historical periods: *mercantilism* (16th-18th centuries — global wealth as static; extraction from colonies); the Industrial Revolution and postindustrial era (pursuit of wealth, "scruples in an electric dynamo" — Tarbell on Rockefeller [V] BT] Ch 5.2); Information Age and Internet age (codes of professional conduct; emphasis shifts from imposed normative rules to individual ethical embrace) [AP] (Ch 5.2, "Shifts in Cultural and Ethical Standards"). The text traces the legal evolution from Dodge v. Ford (1919) through Shlensky v. Wrigley (1968) to Burwell v. Hobby Lobby (2014) (Ch 5.2, "A Matter of Time").

The UN Global Compact is referenced as a voluntary CEO-led initiative to align business with human-rights, labour, environmental, and anticorruption principles (Ch 5.2).

### Geography, religion, and the localisation challenge

*Localisation* is "the process of adapting a product for non-native environments and languages, especially other nations and cultures" [V] (Ch 5.3, "Business as Global Relationships"). Cultural variation in time-perception is documented: Italian *subito*, German *sofort*, Kenyan *tutaonana baadaye*, Tuareg tea ritual, "now now" in some English-speaking African countries (Ch 5.3). "Contact" cultures (South America, Middle East, southern Europe) differ from "noncontact" cultures (northern Europe, Far East, US) in proximity, eye contact, and touch (Ch 5.3).

The text traces religion's historical entanglement with ethics. The Protestant work ethic shaped industrialising Britain and US. Rockefeller is cited as exemplar of the religious-business nexus: he viewed his fortune "as a public trust rather than his personal prize" (Ch 5.3, "Religion and Ethics"). However, Tarbell's work demonstrated his Darwinian, "survival of the fittest" practices in attaining the fortune (Ch 5.3). *Stewardship* — "an attitude toward money and capital that stresses care and responsibility rather than pure utility" — is identified as common across Judaic, Islamic, and Christian traditions (Ch 5.3).

### Universal values, normative business ethics, and humanistic business

The text argues for the existence of *absolute values* that transcend culture and time: "Abstaining from cheating customers, defrauding clients, lying, and murder are fairly objective ethical values" [V] (Ch 5.4, "Absolute Values versus Relative Values"). Ethical systems — utilitarian, rights-based, virtue, natural law — translate absolute values into workable solutions; from these emerges a "basic set of ethical norms for the business world".

Business ethics operates at three levels: individual, organisational, societal. The text references World Economic Forum estimates that "no less than $2 trillion is lost each year worldwide as a result of corruption" [V] (Ch 5.4, "Business Ethics and Compliance"). US companies spend over $70 billion annually on ethics training; worldwide more than double (Ch 5.4). The *Federal Sentencing Guidelines* set out seven steps for organisational compliance: code of ethics, high-level oversight, ethical people in authority, communicate standards, facilitate misconduct reporting, react and respond, prevent (Ch 5.4).

The book takes a pointed position against compliance-as-checkbox: many organisations focus on the letter of the law so they can claim "good faith"; "middle managers and employees often complain their ethics training consists of passing a computerized sexual harassment or fraud program once a year but that nothing is done to address issues in a substantive way or to change the culture of the organization" [V] (Ch 5.4).

The *humanistic business model* is "a business model for balancing profitability and responsibility fairly" by treating employees "as a vital part of the operation" — supporting professional training, healthcare, education, family, spiritual concerns (Ch 5.4, "Normative Business Ethics"). The book suggests adding the *humanities* to business education: literature, history, philosophy. Richard DeGeorge is quoted: "Students do not need psychosociological jargon in their business interactions. They do need to understand people and their motives, to know how to read and judge character, and to have the ability to imagine themselves in another's shoes" [V] [BT] (Ch 5.4).

*Moral agency* is "the self-awareness, freedom, and ability to make choices based on our perception of right and wrong" [V] (Ch 5.4). Bias diminishes moral agency.

## Part VI: What employers owe employees (Ch 6)

### Ethical responsibilities of management

The book frames employer obligations as: "providing a safe workplace, compensating workers fairly, and treating them with a sense of dignity and equality while respecting at least a minimum of their privacy" [V] (Ch 6.1, "Ethical Decision-Making and Leadership in the Workplace"). Managers should model ethical behaviour ("Follow my lead, do as I do" [V] Ch 6.1). The book takes a position against euphemistic language for layoffs: terms like "right sizing" sound like "corporate doublespeak designed to help managers justify, and thereby feel better (and minimize guilt), about their (or the company's) decisions" [V] (Ch 6.1).

The *WARN Act* (Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification, 1989) requires sixty days' advance notice of mass layoffs and plant closings. Pre-WARN, only 20 per cent of displaced workers received written advance notice; "only 7 percent had two months' notice of their impending displacement" [V] [BT] (Ch 6.1).

### Workplace safety, harassment, and satisfaction

OSHA (1970) requires a workplace "free of risk to employees' safety and health" (Ch 6.1, "Workplace Safety under the Occupational Safety and Health Act"). Employer obligations: identify and eliminate hazards, inform employees, train, provide protective gear at no cost, post and maintain records (Ch 6.1). OSHA fines: BP exceeded $102 million from 2005-2009; Union Carbide $2.8 million; Samsung Guam $8.2 million; Imperial Sugar $8.7 million; Walking Dead producers $12,675 (Ch 6.1).

The EEOC enforces Title VII of the Civil Rights Act (1964) prohibiting workplace discrimination including *sexual harassment*. More than thirty thousand harassment complaints are filed each year (Ch 6.1, "A Workplace Free of Harassment"). The text presents harassment as widespread and the law's enforcement as imperfect: Ford settled for $22 million in the 1990s for harassment at two Chicago-area assembly plants and reached a new $10 million settlement with EEOC in August 2017 for similar misconduct at the same plants [AE] (Ch 6.1). Sharon Dunn, a party to both settlements: "For all the good that was supposed to come out of what happened to us, it seems like Ford did nothing. If I had that choice today, I wouldn't say a damn word." [V] (Ch 6.1).

The text takes a strong position on workplace happiness and productivity: "Happy employees are more productive and more focused" (Ch 6.1, "A Satisfied Workforce"). It catalogues practices that create such a workforce: knowing employees as people, recognising progress, asking for and implementing employee ideas, celebrating accomplishments, encouraging exercise and sleep over long work hours, allowing employees to disengage. Germany and France banned work-related employer emails on weekends [AE] (Ch 6.1, citing *Fortune*).

Generational differences in valued benefits: workers 18-35 rank career advancement (32%) and work-life balance (33%) as most important; workers older than 35 — 42% say work-life balance is most important; Generation X values work-life balance; Boomers value salary and retirement; Millennials value flexibility, paid time off, telecommuting, and professional development (Ch 6.1).

### Fair wages and the gender pay gap

The Center for Financial Services Innovation reports "85% of Americans are anxious about their personal financial situation" with stress affecting work performance [V] [BT] (Ch 6.2, "Economic Data as an Indicator of Fair Wages"). Pew Research Center: between 1980 and 2014, inflation-adjusted hourly wages of middle-income workers rose just 6% (less than 0.2% per year); high-income workers' wages rose 41% over the same years. The federal minimum wage is $7.25 per hour; 29 states had higher minima as of late 2017 (Ch 6.2). Since 1960, the inflation-adjusted minimum wage declined 23% [V] (Ch 6.2).

The *gender pay gap*: women in top positions (CEO, VP, general counsel) often earn about 80% of what men with the same titles earn; EEOC 2011-2015 data shows women in senior-level roles earned an average of about $600,000 vs men's $800,000 — a 35% wage gap [V] [BT] (Ch 6.2, "Unfair Wages: The Gender Pay Gap"). In energy industry land-professional roles with similar experience and education, men earned $7000 more per year than women (Ch 6.2). Women earned about 60% of men's wages in 1960, rising to 80% by 2016; pay equity is projected for 2059-2119 [V] (Ch 6.2).

The Equal Pay Act (1963) requires equal pay for the *same* job, but not for *equivalent* jobs. The proposed Paycheck Fairness Act and Fair Pay Act would extend protection to "equivalent" work; *comparable worth* — paying based on worth to the organisation — is central. A starting-salary difference of $5000 with 5% annual raises compounds to $600,000+ over a 40-year career; women must work three years longer to earn the same lifetime amount (Ch 6.2).

Iceland (2018) "makes it illegal to pay men more than women, gauged not by specific job category, but rather in all jobs collectively at any employer with twenty-five or more employees" [V] (Ch 6.2, "European Approaches to the Gender Pay Gap"). 65% of OECD countries have introduced new pay-equality policies. UK requires pay-gap publication for employers with 250+ workers (Ch 6.2).

Eight US states ban employers from asking job applicants their salary history — addressing how prior pay perpetuates the gap (Ch 6.2).

### The Gravity Payments and Pharmalogics experiments

The book takes a notable position on creative compensation. Dan Price at Gravity Payments (Seattle, 2015) phased in a $70,000 minimum salary, reducing his own pay from $1 million to $70,000. Five thousand applied for jobs the following week. Gravity's retention rate is 91%. Price calls the minimum wage "an ethical and moral imperative rather than a business strategy" [V] (Ch 6.2, "Experiments in Compensation"). Megan Driscoll at Pharmalogics raised starting base pay 33% — staff grew from 46 to 72, revenues from $6.7 million to $15 million (Ch 6.2). Stephan Aarstol at Tower Paddleboards cut work day to five hours from ten, doubling effective pay; engagement and profits skyrocketed (Ch 6.2).

The text observes US productivity rose 22% since 2000 while inflation-adjusted median wages rose only 2%; "a larger share of capitalism's rewards are going to shareholders and top executives (who already earn an average of three hundred times more than typical workers, up from seventy times more just a decade ago)" [V] (Ch 6.2).

### Organised labour, codetermination, and the pay ratio

Union pros: bargaining power, dispute resolution, lobbying, industry-wide norms. Cons: harder to fast-track promotions or remove low performers, dues, less diversity, adversarial relationships (Ch 6.3, "Organized Labor", Table 6.1). Union membership peaked at 33% in the 1950s; total US union membership dropped to 20% of the workforce by 1980 and to about 10% by 2016. Public-sector membership: 35%; private sector: 6.5% (Ch 6.3). Highest state: New York at 23%; lowest: South Carolina just over 1%.

*Right-to-work laws* exist in 28 states and give workers the option not to join the union even at firms where the majority voted union (Ch 6.3). *Closed shop* is a union shop where new hires must join the union and dues are deducted from pay automatically.

*Codetermination* — "a workplace concept that goes beyond unionization to embrace shared governance, in which management and workers cooperate in decision-making and workers have the right to participate on the board of directors" [V] (Ch 6.3) — applies to companies over a certain size in EU countries (Germany: 500+ employees). The book takes a positive position on codetermination: in Germany it has produced "more engaged" workers, "fewer strikes, better pay, and safer working conditions" — "a classic win-win for both sides" [V] (Ch 6.3).

International unionisation rates (Ch 6.3, Table 6.2): Sweden 82, Italy 35, Canada 30, UK 29, Germany 26, Australia 25, Japan 22, US 12, France 9.

US productivity grew 74% from 1973 to 2016 (OECD data); wages grew 12%; "productivity has grown six times more than pay" [V] (Ch 6.3, "Pay and Productivity in the United States"). In global productivity rankings, the US is among the top five or six (OECD ranks the US second).

The *pay ratio* — CEO pay as a multiple of average employee pay — is approximately 300 in the US, 22 in UK, 15 in France, 12 in Germany; 1965 US ratio was 20 (Ch 6.3, "Is Management Compensation Fair?"). Between 1978 and 2014, "inflation-adjusted CEO pay increased by almost 1,000 percent in the United States, while worker pay rose 11 percent" [V] [BT] (Ch 6.3).

### Workplace privacy and monitoring

The Electronic Communications Privacy Act of 1986 (ECPA) regulates monitoring. The *business purpose exception* allows employers to monitor "on the basis of legitimate business purposes"; the *consent exception* allows monitoring with employee consent (Ch 6.4, "Legal and Ethical Aspects of Electronic Monitoring"). Employers may monitor stored emails on their own systems but face limits on stored emails generally. State laws vary; California and Pennsylvania require both-party consent for recorded conversations.

A 2010 American Management Association survey: 48% of employers used video monitoring; 67% monitored Internet use; 30% had terminated an employee based on electronic monitoring (Ch 6.4). *Ontario v. Quon* (2010, US Supreme Court, unanimous) held that constitutional limits on unreasonable searches by public employers were "minimal given a work-related purpose" (Ch 6.4).

*Employment at will* — "either the employee or the employer may dissolve the employment arrangement at will (i.e., without cause and at any time unless an employment contract is in effect that stipulates differently)" [V] (Ch 6, "Key Terms") — covers approximately 85% of US private-sector employees (Ch 6.4, "Drug Testing in the Workplace"). Drug testing is legal in most states; employees may refuse but typically lose the job. Mark Rothstein's research catalogues legal challenges based on intrusive procedures: a court found unlawful retaliation when employees were dismissed for inability to urinate while observed; another awarded $125,000 for invasion of privacy and emotional distress to a worker forced to provide an observed urine sample (Ch 6.4).

## Part VII: What employees owe employers (Ch 7)

### Duty of loyalty, confidentiality, non-compete agreements

The *duty of loyalty* requires that "an employee refrain from acting in a manner contrary to the employer's interest" [V] (Ch 7, "Key Terms"; Ch 7.1, "A Duty of Loyalty"). The duty is rooted in common law of agency and rises to *fiduciary duty* at higher levels of trust. A graphic designer at an advertising agency cannot moonlight on weekends for a friend's web design business but might do so for a friend's catering business or wedding photography (Ch 7.1).

The book observes that "loyalty is a two-way street" — employers' preference for at-will employment naturally reduces employee loyalty (Ch 7.1, "Differing Concepts of Loyalty"). Millennials are three times more likely than older generations to change jobs; 91% do not expect to stay with their current job longer than three years [V] [BT] (Ch 7.1). The shift from defined-benefit to defined-contribution retirement plans rewards high earnings over seniority and reduces loyalty incentives (Ch 7.1).

The *duty of confidentiality* covers proprietary information — trade secrets, intellectual property, employee records, customer data (Ch 7.1, "Confidentiality"). Federal IP law protects trademarks, copyrights, and patents; *trade secrets* are protected by company secrecy and *nondisclosure agreements* (Ch 7.1).

*Non-compete agreements* limit competition during and after employment. About 20% of the US private-sector workforce, and one in six workers earning under $40,000, are now covered by non-compete agreements [V] (Ch 7.1). California (2017) declared most post-employment non-competes void. The Jimmy John's case: New York AG Eric Schneiderman pressured Jimmy John's to drop a clause prohibiting employees from working at any business selling "submarine, hero-type, deli-style, pita, and/or wrapped or rolled sandwiches" within two miles of any Jimmy John's anywhere in the US (more than 2,500 stores in 46 states) [V] [AE] (Ch 7.1, "Non-Compete Agreements"). Schneiderman: "They limit mobility and opportunity for vulnerable workers and bully them into staying with the threat of being sued." [V] (Ch 7.1).

*Nonsolicitation clauses* protect employers from departing employees luring customers or colleagues; *nondisparagement clauses* prohibit defaming the former employer.

### Brand loyalty, customers, and respect

Employees should help in *branding* — "creating, differentiating, and maintaining a brand's image or reputation" [V] (Ch 7.2, "Respecting the Brand"). American Express's mantra: "Happy employees make happy customers" [V] (Ch 7.2). Negative employee actions on social media damage brands swiftly: Taco Bell employee licking tacos; Domino's Pizza employee placing cheese in his nose then in food; Burger King employee posting photo lying on hamburger buns (Ch 7.2). *Internal marketing* is "the process of getting employees to believe in the company's product and even to buy it" [V] (Ch 7, "Key Terms").

Employees owe customers respect; the Pizza Hut/Lockard case is presented as a counterpoint: a server who was harassed and physically assaulted by repeat customers was instructed by her boss to keep waiting on them; a jury awarded her $360,000 (Ch 7.2). The book takes a clear position: employees do not owe customers tolerance of harassment.

### Workplace atmosphere, codes of conduct, workplace violence

The book lists practices for getting along with coworkers: keep an open mind, be kind, respect, avoid sexual jokes, work with difficult people professionally, do not gossip on social media (Ch 7.3, "Getting Along with Coworkers"). *Workplace personality* (how we think and act on the job) and *work style* (how we accomplish tasks) vary; the text recommends emotional intelligence and empathy (Ch 7.3, "Understanding Personalities").

Four NIOSH categories of workplace violence: traditional criminal intent, worker-on-worker (~15% of homicides), personal relationship (~10%, women at higher risk), customer/patient (~20% in 2014; concentrated in nightclubs, restaurants, healthcare) [BT] (Ch 7.3, "Reducing Workplace Violence").

Sample code of conduct topics (Ch 7.3, Table 7.1): compliance with all laws, corruption and fraud, conflict of interest, company property, cybersecurity and digital devices, social media, sexual harassment, workplace respect.

### Financial integrity: insider trading and bribery

*Insider trading* is "the buying or selling of stocks, bonds, or other investments based on nonpublic information that is likely to affect the price of the security being traded" [V] (Ch 7, "Key Terms"). The Securities Exchange Act of 1934 Section 10(b)5 and SEC actions create the legal framework. Insiders include officers, directors, employees, and "temporary insiders" (investment bankers, brokers, attorneys, accountants, journalists) who acquire confidential information through a unique relationship with a firm (Ch 7.4, "Insider Trading").

The Texas Gulf Sulphur case (1968) is foundational: mining employees bought stock after learning of the Kidd Mine copper deposit, sold after public disclosure, and were forced to disgorge profits (Ch 7.4). The Drexel Burnham Lambert / Boesky / Levine / Siegel / Milken case (1980s): Boesky paid Levine and Siegel for pre-takeover details. Penalties: Milken paid $1.1 billion total ($200 million government fines, $400 million to investors, $500 million to DBL clients) and was sentenced to 10 years in prison and a lifetime industry ban; Boesky 3.5 years and $100 million; Levine $11.5 million plus $2 million back taxes and 2 years in prison; DBL ultimately failed in 1990 [BT] (Ch 7.4, "Insider Trading and Fiduciary Duty").

A *bribe* is "a payment in some form (cash or noncash) for an act that runs counter to the legal and ethical culture of the work environment" [V] (Ch 7, "Key Terms"). The *Foreign Corrupt Practices Act* (FCPA, 1977; amended 1988, 1998) prohibits bribery of foreign officials. The *nationality principle* extends jurisdiction broadly. Examples: BHP Billiton and GlaxoSmithKline each paid $25 million for buying foreign officials tickets to the 2008 Beijing Olympics; Telia paid $965 million for FCPA violations connected to bribery in Uzbekistan (Ch 7.4, "Bribery and the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act"). The 1997 OECD Anti-Bribery Convention has 43 signatories. UK Bribery Act 2010, Canada's Corruption of Foreign Officials Act 1999, and the OECD Convention on Combating Bribery of Foreign Public Officials parallel the FCPA.

Federal employee gift policy (offered as model): no gift to a superior on annual occasions exceeding $10; no solicitation/acceptance of gifts because of official position; gifts under $20 with reasonable frequency; no expensive restaurant entertainment; report total gifts from one source exceeding $390 per year (Ch 7.4).

### Pay secrecy, criticism, and whistleblowing

Ten US states (California, Colorado, Illinois, Louisiana, Maine, Michigan, Minnesota, New Hampshire, New Jersey, Vermont) have banned *pay secrecy* (Ch 7.5, "Limiting Pay Secrecy"). The 2012 NLRB and federal court decisions made most pay-secrecy policies unenforceable under the National Labor Relations Act of 1935; protections cover union and non-union employees and extend to social-media discussions of pay.

The text takes a definite position: the First Amendment does *not* protect at-will employees who criticise their boss or company on social media (Ch 7.5, "Speaking Out on Social Media"). The Adrian Duane / IXL Learning case is reviewed: NLRB judge Etchingham held a Glassdoor post was not "concerted activity" under NLRA Section 7 — described it as "a tantrum" and "childish ridicule" [V] [BT] (Ch 7.5).

*Whistleblowing* is "the act of reporting an employer to a governmental entity for violating the law" [V] (Ch 7, "Key Terms"). The text's ethical guidance: appropriate motive (compliance, victim protection), not revenge; try internal channels first; have hard evidence; the violation should be serious; whistleblowing should have "some likelihood of stopping the wrongful act" (Ch 7.5, "Whistleblowing: Risks and Rewards"). The Occupational Safety and Health Act (1970) was an early law with whistleblower protection; today over 20 federal laws protect specific categories.

The *False Claims Act* (1863, 31 U.S.C. §§ 3729-3733) — passed during the Civil War — has *qui tam* provisions allowing private "relators" to file suits on behalf of the government and receive 15-25% of recovery (or 25-30% if government does not intervene). Notable awards: Bradley Birkenfeld (former UBS) — $104 million from IRS in 2012 ("the most highly rewarded whistleblower in history"); ten former Pfizer employees — $102 million combined in 2009 ($50 million to John Kopchinski); HCA Medicare-fraud whistleblowers — $100 million combined [V] [BT] (Ch 7.5).

The Sherron Watkins / Enron case is presented as a cautionary illustration: Watkins reported fraud internally to chairman Ken Lay, was ignored, then went to the press — "Because she did not go directly to the SEC, Watkins received no whistleblower protection" (Ch 7.5, "Sherron Watkins and Enron"). Watkins: "I will never have a job in corporate America again. The minute you speak truth to power and you're not heard, your career is never the same again." [V] (Ch 7.5).

The Sallie Krawcheck / Merrill Lynch case illustrates the moral cost even of internal protected speech: Krawcheck successfully argued for compensating Stable Value Fund investors (many Walmart employees) and won her CEO's backing — "speaking out did come at a cost. Krawcheck lost some important and powerful allies within the company, and although she did not lose her job at that time ... 'before long I was 'reorganized out' of that role.'" [V] (Ch 7.5).

## Part VIII: Recognising the rights of all (Ch 8)

### Workforce diversity and the diversity dividend

The text frames *diversity* as more than gender and ethnicity: "different thinking styles and personality types, as well as other factors such as physical and cognitive abilities and sexual orientation" [V] [BT] (Ch 8.1, "Workplace Diversity", citing SHRM). *Inclusion* is "the engagement of all employees in the corporate culture" — Howard Ross: "Diversity is being invited to the party; inclusion is being asked to dance." [V] [BT] (Ch 8.1).

McKinsey & Company found a positive correlation between gender/ethnic diversity and financial performance — the *diversity dividend* (Ch 8.1, "Adding Value through Diversity"). Companies in the top 25% for gender diversity were 15% more likely to post above-median financial returns; for racial/ethnic diversity, 35% more likely. McKinsey examined 20,000 firms in 90 countries — top 25% for executive/board diversity had returns on equity 50% higher than bottom 25% [V] [BT] (Ch 8.1).

US workforce demographics: women's labour-force participation rose from 48% in 1977 to a peak of 60% in 1999 (steady at 57% recently); from 41 million to 71 million women, projected to 92 million by 2050 (Ch 8.1). Hispanic share projected to rise from 15% (2010) to 30% (2050); Asian from 5% to 8%. Technology sector lags: Google about 30% women overall, fewer than 20% in technical roles; about 56% White, 35% Asian, 3.5% Latino, 2.4% Black; 68% of management roles held by White people [V] [BT] (Ch 8.1, citing Google diversity report).

Backlash is documented: a 2017 Google software engineer's leaked memo attributing women's underrepresentation to "biological causes" and "men's higher drive for status"; the engineer was fired and filed a class-action discrimination lawsuit on behalf of "Whites, conservatives, and men"; the NLRB ruled in February 2018 that Google's termination did not violate federal labor law [BT] (Ch 8.1).

### Reasonable accommodation: ability and faith

The Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA, 1990) defines disability and requires *reasonable accommodation* unless it imposes *undue hardship* — "significant difficulty or expense" [V] (Ch 8.2, "Protections for People with Disabilities"). Accommodations: physical workplace alteration, job restructuring, equipment modification, part-time/modified schedules, readers, interpreters, assistive animals.

The Verizon EEOC settlement (2011): $20 million for an inflexible "no-fault" attendance policy that disciplined or fired employees for absences caused by their disabilities — "the largest single disability discrimination settlement in the agency's history" (Ch 8.2). Verizon was required to change the policy and provide regular ADA training to attendance-policy managers.

Title VII of the CRA prohibits religious discrimination. Four tenets: nondiscrimination, nonharassment, nonretaliation, reasonable accommodation (Ch 8.2, "Managing Religious Diversity in the Workplace"). *Welsh v. United States* (1970) extended protection to nontheistic value systems "occupying a place parallel to that filled by the God of those admittedly qualifying for the exception" [V] (Ch 8.2). The Abercrombie & Fitch case: Samantha Elauf was denied a job for wearing a hijab (against the company's "Look Policy"); EEOC general counsel David Lopez: "This case is about defending the American principles of religious freedom and tolerance." [V] (Ch 8.2, "The Abercrombie & Fitch Religious Discrimination Case").

### Sexual identification and orientation

The text traces legal protection through *United States v. Windsor* (2013), *Obergefell v. Hodges* (2015), and *Bostock v. Clayton County* (2020) — the Court held discrimination based on "sex" includes discrimination based on sexual orientation and gender identity (Ch 8.3, "Legal Protections"). Denver Investments analysed stock performance before and after companies adopted LGBTQ-inclusive workplace policies and found post-adoption outperformance [BT] (Ch 8.3, "Ethical Considerations").

Backlash legislation: Indiana's Religious Freedom Restoration Act and North Carolina's H.B. 2 ("bathroom bill") drew consumer boycotts and corporate opposition. In 2016, nearly seventy large US companies (American Airlines, Apple, DuPont, GE, IBM, Morgan Stanley, Walmart) signed an amicus brief opposing H.B. 2 (Ch 8.3). The Human Rights Campaign Corporate Equality Index scores ~1000 large US companies; over 600 earned a perfect score in 2018 (AT&T, Boeing, Coca-Cola, Gap, GM, Johnson & Johnson, Kellogg, UPS, Xerox among them) (Ch 8.3).

### Income inequality and pay equity

The University of California economic research: "the top 10 percent of income earners averaging ten times as much income as the bottom 90 percent, and the top 1 percent making more than forty times what the bottom 90 percent does" [V] [BT] (Ch 8.4, "The Middle Class in the United States"). Top 1% share of US income rose from 8% to 22% over 35 years. Middle-income share of US households shrank from ~60% in 1970 to 47% in 2014.

Robert Reich quotes a CEO: "Because the American middle class is the core of our customer base. If they can't afford our products in the years ahead, we're in deep trouble." [V] [BT] (Ch 8.4, "Addressing Income Inequality"). Bill Gross and others advocate raising the federal minimum wage. Lloyd Blankfein (Goldman Sachs CEO) is quoted on income inequality: "It is destabilizing the nation and is responsible for the divisions in the country" [V] [BT] (Ch 8.4).

The book registers the contrary view of David Neumark and William L. Wascher: "we are hard-pressed to imagine a compelling argument for a higher minimum wage when it neither helps low-income families nor reduces poverty" [V] [BT] (Ch 8.4). They favour Earned Income Tax Credit and other targeted approaches.

29 states have minimum wages exceeding the federal $7.25; cities like San Francisco and Seattle reach $15.00. Voluntarily raised wages: Costco, T.J. Maxx, Marshalls, IKEA, Starbucks, Gap, In-and-Out Burger, Whole Foods, Ben & Jerry's, Shake Shack, McDonald's, Target ($11/hr), Wells Fargo / PNC / Fifth Third Bank ($15/hr) (Ch 8.4).

The American Sustainable Business Council survey of 500+ small businesses found 58-66% supported a minimum wage of at least $10/hour. Frank Knapp, South Carolina Small Business Chamber: "will put more money in the hands of 300,000 South Carolinians ... and they will spend it here in our local economies" [V] [BT] (Ch 8.4).

Department of Labor data: total employer compensation cost averaged $35.64/hour (Sept 2017); wages/salaries 68% ($24.33), benefits 32% ($11.31). The 30% saved by hiring contractors instead of employees "is often the margin between being in the middle class and falling below it" (Ch 8.4).

The New Jersey Diane B. Allen Equal Pay Act and Minnesota's state-employee law require "equal pay for substantially similar work" rather than "equal pay for equal work" — addressing the use of slightly different job titles to justify pay differences (Ch 8.4, "Pay Equity as a Corollary of Income Equality"). The World Economic Forum has documented that gender inequality is strongly associated with income inequality across 140 countries over 20 years (Ch 8.4).

### Animal rights

The text traces the animal-rights movement through the ASPCA, American Humane Association, American Welfare Institute, HSUS; the Humane Slaughter Act (1950s); the Animal Welfare Act (1966) (Ch 8.5, "A Brief History of the Animal Rights Movement"). Rhode Island, Boulder, Berkeley have enacted laws recognising people as guardians rather than owners of animals.

*Agribusiness ethics* span ecology, animal rights, economics, food safety, sustainability. The text observes that food production must double over the next 50 years to meet demand (Ch 8.5, "The Ethics of What We Eat"). The book lists the dietary trends transforming agribusiness: vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, non-GMO; ethical, religious (halal, kosher) considerations (Ch 8.5).

Biomedical and cosmetics testing alternatives: patient-drug databases, virtual drug trials, computer models, MRI/CT imaging, microdosing, in vitro human cell cultures, stem cells, genetic testing (Ch 8.5, "The Use of Animals in Medical and Cosmetic Research"). Laws: Stephen Latham's continuum from strong UK regulation to Japanese self-regulation (US in the middle). The Nonhuman Rights Project's habeas-corpus suits on behalf of chimpanzees Tommy and Kiko have been unsuccessful (Ch 8.5).

The EU (2013), Norway, Switzerland, Guatemala, India, Israel, New Zealand, South Korea, Taiwan, Turkey have banned or limited cosmetics animal testing. The US Humane Cosmetics Act has not passed Congress (Ch 8.5). According to APPA, two-thirds of US households own a pet; pet industry sales tripled in 15 years and were projected to reach ~$70 billion by 2018 (Ch 8.5).

## Part IX: Professions under the microscope (Ch 9)

### Entrepreneurship and start-up culture

Entrepreneurship's failure rate is high: business consultant Patrick Henry reports "75 percent of venture-backed startups fail" citing a Harvard Business School study by Shikhar Ghosh; Statistical Brain found U.S. failure rates over 50% after five years and 70% after ten [V] [BT] (Ch 9.1, "The Risks of Entrepreneurship").

The book takes a strong position on entrepreneur character. The Uber / Travis Kalanick case is treated at length: the *New York Times* depicted Uber's culture as "aggressive, unrestrained workplace culture" [V] (Ch 9.1) including misogyny, homophobia, and harassment as documented by Susan Fowler's blog; Kalanick was forced out by the board in June 2017.

Max Weber on bureaucracy is invoked: "in the private economy, [bureaucracy emerges] only in the most advanced institutions of capitalism" [V] [BT] (Ch 9.1, "Why Successful Start-Ups Change as They Grow", citing *Wirtschaft und Gesellschaft*). And: "Once it is firmly established, bureaucracy is among those social structures which are the hardest to destroy." [V] [BT] (Ch 9.1).

*Entrepreneurial culture* is "the combination of personality and management style with which entrepreneurs shape the initial business practices and ethical environment of their firm" [V] (Ch 9, "Key Terms"). The Paul Orfalea / Kinko's case is offered as a leadership confession: Orfalea grew Kinko's from one store (1970) to 1,200 stores and 23,000 employees over 25 years before selling to FedEx; he reflects that "I don't hide the fact that I have a problem with anger" [V] (Ch 9.1, "Growing Up with a Start-Up"). His advice to entrepreneurs: do not give way to anger, do not take it home, "try to be the person you most genuinely are, both at work and at home".

### The influence of advertising

The book frames *psychological appeals* — advertising "intended to bolster consumers' self-esteem if certain products or services are purchased" [V] (Ch 9, "Key Terms") — as the most successful ad approach (Ch 9.2, "Does Advertising Drive Us to Unnecessary Purchases?"). John Kenneth Galbraith's *dependence effect* (from *The Affluent Society*, 1958) is cited: corporations harness advertising to influence consumer purchasing decisions in ways that may produce frivolous demand [BT] (Ch 9.2).

The Pet Rock craze (1977-, retailing at $19.95 in 2017) is offered as an example of marketing creating demand for products with minimal usefulness (Ch 9.2). Children are particularly susceptible to advertising — they have "difficulty separating what is real on television from what is not" [V] (Ch 9.2). Young children (under 7-8 years) view advertising as credibly as information from trustworthy adults.

*Subliminal advertising* is "embedded words or images that allegedly reach us only beneath the level of our consciousness" [V] (Ch 9, "Key Terms"). Vance Packard's *The Hidden Persuaders* (1957) reported on James Vicary's claim of cinema-based subliminal messaging — the data was self-promotional and dismissed; the "scientific evidence establishing any real phenomenon of subliminal advertising is inconclusive" (Ch 9.2). A 2015 University of South Carolina study found thirsty subjects could be subliminally influenced in beverage choice if primed within 15 minutes of acknowledging thirst, with effects receding after that window (Ch 9.2).

### The insurance industry

Insurance arose from 17th-century maritime trade syndicates; the first such association began at Samuel Lloyd's coffee house in 1688 (origin of Lloyd's of London) (Ch 9.3, "How Insurance Works"). The insurance industry's foundational ethical quandary: insurers profit when premiums are numerous and claims few. "What constitutes a reasonable profit" is the ethical question.

Insurance does not cover all losses. After Hurricane Harvey (Aug 2017, 52 inches of rain on Houston), of "over $30 billion in property losses in Texas, only 40 percent may be insured" [V] [BT] (Ch 9.3). Flood insurance is a federal add-on (FEMA); the National Flood Insurance Act of 1968 created the federal mechanism. The California Earthquake Authority manages privately-funded earthquake insurance at state level. About 5 million households had FEMA flood coverage as of August 2017 (Ch 9.3).

Climate change creates an emerging insurance problem; California Insurance Commissioner Dave Jones warned in December 2017 that "insurers may start to back off writing insurance in some areas" after a particularly disastrous fire season (Ch 9.3, "The Ethical Dilemma of Insuring against Natural Disasters").

*Redlining* — "a discriminatory (and usually illegal) insurance practice of denying certain coverages in specific neighborhoods or selling them there at a higher price" [V] (Ch 9, "Key Terms") — was the subject of a 2017 Consumer Reports / ProPublica study of auto insurance in California, Illinois, Missouri, Texas. The study found "many of the disparities in auto insurance prices between minority and White neighborhoods are wider than differences in risk can explain" [V] [BT] (Ch 9.3, "Redlining: Discrimination in Insurance"). Carriers including Allstate, Geico, and Liberty Mutual were named; the ethical line is that race or ethnicity must play no role in premium-setting.

### Health care provision

The US has a *multipayer health care system* — patient, employer, private insurer all contribute (Germany, France, Japan also multipayer). UK and Canada use *single-payer health care systems* — government as sole payer funded by tax revenues (Ch 9.4, "Multipayer Health Care in the United States"). The Commonwealth Fund: "the United States spends far more on health care than other high income countries ... Yet the U.S. population has poorer health than other countries" [V] [BT] (Ch 9.4).

William B. Schultz: "in the past 35 years, the only significant victory in the battle to control drug prices has been the enactment of legislation that established the generic drug program at the FDA" [V] [BT] (Ch 9.4, "The High Cost of Prescription Drugs"). Prescription drug prices accounted for 17% of US healthcare costs in recent years, up from 7% in the 1990s; nearly 20% of Medicare program spending. The pharmaceutical industry argues for high drug prices as recovery of R&D costs; critics cite marketing expense.

The text traces health-coverage history: industrial sickness funds emerged in the late 19th and early 20th centuries; charity (religious organisations); the AMA opposed government health insurance from 1920 (Ch 9.4, "Paying for Health Care and Wellness", citing John E. Murray).

*Wellness programs* aim to reduce health-insurance costs through preventive measures — smoking cessation, gym memberships, healthy food options (Ch 9.4). Michael Hiltzik's caution: "voluntary wellness programs also give employers a window into their workers' health profiles that is otherwise an illegal invasion of their privacy" [V] [BT] (Ch 9.4).

The *Affordable Care Act (ACA, 2010)* — "Obamacare" — mandates preventive care, choice of physicians, coverage for pre-existing conditions, mental health/substance abuse coverage, marketplace plans (Ch 9.4, "The Affordable Care Act"). Massachusetts's 2006 An Act Providing Access to Affordable, Quality, Accountable Health Care preceded the ACA — Brian Mooney in the *Boston Globe*: "the overhaul, after five years, worked as well as or better than expected" [V] [BT] (Ch 9.4, "State-Level Experiments with Single-Payer Health Care Plans"). California's proposed Healthy California Act (SB 562) — a single-payer plan that would cost $370-400 billion and require federal waivers — has been blocked.

The book ends the chapter with a statement of the ethical question: is health care a basic human right? "This is a foundational ethical question that would invoke different responses on the part of nearly everyone you may ask." [V] (Ch 9.4).

## Part X: Changing work environments and future trends (Ch 10)

### Telecommuting

*Telecommuting* is "working from a remote location (home or other space) by means of electronic connections" [V] (Ch 10, "Key Terms"). Almost 4 million US employees skip the commute for at least part of each week; one in five workers globally telecommutes frequently, ~10% from home every day (Ch 10.1, "Telecommuting and Its Advantages").

Productivity evidence: a Harvard Business Review call-center study found telecommuting employees "made 13.5 percent more calls, resigned their positions at only half the usual rate, and had much higher job satisfaction" [V] [BT] (Ch 10.1). Colorado DOT permit-issuance: 48% faster turnaround, 5% more calls. JD Edwards teleworkers: 20-25% more productive. American Express home-workers: 43% more productive than in-office workers. The average US employee saves more than $1000/year on gas (commuting 30 miles/60 minutes per day).

Drawbacks: collaboration loss (Yahoo's Marissa Mayer 2013 work-from-home reversal; IBM, Aetna, Bank of America followed); difficulty conveying corporate culture; harder to monitor; manager-bias against remote workers — "promotions and important projects may go to employees who are more visible" (Ch 10.1, "The Drawbacks of Telecommuting").

### Workplace campuses

Campus-style workplaces are emerging at Apple, Microsoft, Facebook, Amazon, Alphabet/Google with "recreation centers, fine dining, parks, walking and biking paths, climbing walls, free snacks, child-care facilities, basketball courts, haircuts and massages, and laundry, dry-cleaning, and car wash operations" (Ch 10.2, "Workplace Campuses and Historical Precedents"). Facebook's Willow Village (Menlo Park, ~1500 units) plans to reserve 15% for low-income neighbours. Google's Googleplex herb garden teaches Googlers about food (Ch 10.2). Acuity Insurance (Sheboygan, Wisconsin) features a working Ferris wheel and theater-in-the-round seating 2000 (Ch 10.2, "Acuity's Workplace Campus").

The book draws a parallel to 19th-century company towns owned by employers (Pullman, Ford). Critics note that company towns produce a *monopsony* in labour markets: "a monopsonist in the labor market can leverage its market power. Because it is the only firm hiring, it can pay its workers less." [V] (Ch 10.2). The book takes a cautious position: corporate campuses risk tethering workers to jobs and reducing labour mobility.

### Job sharing, flextime, gig economy

*Job sharing* uses two or more employees to perform one full-time position (Ch 10.3, "Job Sharing and Flextime"). Pros: less absenteeism, planning around family obligations, retention of experienced employees, on-the-job training. Cons: "two Mondays effect" (productivity loss as each partner gets up to speed); coordination expense; employer use of part-time positions to avoid benefits.

*Flextime* allows employees to choose start/finish times. Bias against late-start workers exists — managers "incorrectly regard people who prioritize an early start as more desirable employees" (Ch 10.3).

The *access economy* (peer-to-peer, P2P) "is essentially a resource circulation system in which consumers participate on both sides of a transaction" [V] (Ch 10, "Key Terms"). Airbnb US hosts in San Francisco rent ~60 nights/year and earn ~$10,000; RelayRides car owners earn ~$250/month (Ch 10.3, "The Access Economy and Online Platforms"). The 2012 JOBS Act enabled crowdfunding-based capital raising.
