The *gig economy* "is an environment in which individuals and businesses contract with independent workers for short-term assignments, engagements, or projects, offering few or no benefits beyond compensation" [V] (Ch 10, "Key Terms"). Intuit projected 40% of US workers would be independent contractors by 2020; the Freelancers Union reports 55+ million US adults already work as independent contractors and/or moonlighters (35% of US workforce). The Freelancers Union has 375,000+ members. Robert Reich is quoted: "This on-demand economy means a work life that is unpredictable, doesn't pay very well, and is terribly insecure." [V] [BT] (Ch 10.3).

The Microsoft case is a cautionary tale: Microsoft saved on benefits and payroll taxes by classifying many workers as contractors but lost a 2000 settlement of $97 million for misclassification. Today Microsoft has ~110,000 employees with 75% temporary or contract workers, but requires staffing companies to provide benefits.

The text takes a strong position on internships: "many interns clearly function as a source of unpaid labor. Ethical boundaries are often crossed, even if students are willing to undertake these positions ... responsible companies will insist that their interns are paid for their services or receive academic credit, or both" [V] (Ch 10.3).

### Robotics and artificial intelligence

A McKinsey Global Institute study of 800 occupations across 50 countries: more than 800 million jobs (20% of global workforce) could be lost to robotics by 2030; up to one-third of US/German workforce affected; 39-73 million jobs eliminated in the US (Ch 10.4, "Robotics and Automation in the Workplace"). India: only ~9% of jobs lost (lower wage levels reduce automation savings). Most affected: machine operators, factory workers, food workers, mortgage brokers, paralegals, accountants, cashiers, toll booth operators, drivers. BLS projects 80,000 fast-food jobs disappear by 2024; 7.5 million retail jobs at risk; ~5 million driving jobs (Ch 10.4).

Less affected by automation: human-interaction jobs — nurses, most physicians, lawyers, teachers, bartenders, social workers (BLS predicts 19% growth by 2024), hairstylists, youth coaches, songwriters, gardeners, plumbers, care workers (Ch 10.4).

A former McDonald's CEO is quoted: "It's cheaper to buy a $35,000 robotic arm than it is to hire an employee who's inefficiently making $15 an hour bagging French fries" [V] [BT] (Ch 10.4).

*Artificial intelligence (AI)* is "the branch of science that uses computer algorithms to replicate human intelligent behavior by machines with minimal human intervention" [V] (Ch 10, "Key Terms"). Accenture frames AI as "the next great advance in the workplace ... fundamentally reinventing how businesses run" [V] [BT] (Ch 10.4, "Artificial Intelligence"); estimates AI could "double rates of economic growth by 2035, increase labor productivity by as much as 40 percent". KPMG estimates ~50% of workplace activities could be automated with existing technology.

The text raises governance questions: should companies be required to inform customers when they are interacting with AI rather than humans? Reuters reports EU lawmakers "have agreed on the need for [European Union]-wide legislation that would regulate robots and their use, including an ethical framework for their development and deployment, as well as the establishment of liability for the actions of robots, including self-driving cars" [V] [BT] (Ch 10.4).

## Part XI: Becoming an ethical professional (Ch 11)

### Core thesis: ethics as ongoing formation

The book's epilogue is paraphrased from the lead author's perspective. It defines becoming an ethical professional as a developmental process: "the formation of conscience, and the emergence of an individual in whom being and doing are comfortably aligned, take place over time. There are no shortcuts" [V] (Ch 11, "Introduction"). The Rajat Gupta case — McKinsey managing director, Goldman Sachs board director, convicted in 2009-2012 of insider trading and conspiracy (two years in jail, $5 million in fines) — exemplifies the failure mode (Ch 11.1).

The General Motors ignition-switch case: "The faulty switches caused 124 deaths and 273 injuries, and GM was finally brought to federal court. In 2014, the company reached a settlement for $900 million and recalled 2.6 million cars" [V] [BT] (Ch 11.1, "General Motors' Failure to Consider Stakeholders").

### Ethics in evolving environments

The book identifies cybercrimes and privacy as the dominant 21st-century ethical issues (Ch 11.1). Google's choice in China is offered as a complex case: Google's stated mission is to make information "universally accessible and useful" [V] (Ch 11.1) but Google agreed to Chinese government censorship demands in 2006, withdrew in 2010, and as of 2018 remains largely banned (Gmail, YouTube, Maps, Search). The Cheesecake Factory's Hong Kong outlet illustrates a tension between market wants and customer needs — the book asks whether Western companies imposing standardised products elsewhere "would be a form of imperialism" (Ch 11.1).

### Relationships, values, and the right fit

The chapter takes a strong position on workplace relationships and ethics: "employees are more likely to be unethical when they are dissatisfied with their jobs or see their superiors acting unethically" [V] [BT] (Ch 11.2, "Relationships Matter"). A good example at the top, work-life balance, and positive employee relationships generate a more ethical workplace.

The text catalogues the elements of finding an ethical employer: examining mission, leadership, culture; reading "100 Best Companies to Work For" lists; recognising that even ethical organisations make mistakes (Ch 11.3, "Finding the 'Right Fit'"). Costco is held up as a positive example — insurance for part-time workers, above-industry-average pay, 9.58/10 employee satisfaction (Ch 11.3, Figure 11.5).

The Bill Swanson / Raytheon case is a cautionary tale: Swanson published *Swanson's Unwritten Rules of Management* and was widely cited until plagiarism was discovered; the booklet was discontinued and his retirement package was reduced (Ch 11.3, "Swanson's Rule #1: Don't Plagiarize"). The book quotes the Ethisphere Institute "World's Most Ethical Companies" — UPS earned net income of $4.91 billion in 2017 and Xerox $195 million; both are over a century old and have appeared on the list 11 consecutive years (Ch 11.3).

### Identity, mission statements, and entrepreneurial values

The chapter recommends writing a personal mission statement. Examples cited: Richard Branson — "to have fun in [my] journey through life and learn from [my] mistakes"; Denise Morrison (Campbell Soup CEO) — "to serve as a leader, live a balanced life, and apply ethical principles to make a significant difference" (Ch 11.4, "Identifying Your Values and Mission"). The Rabbi Hillel quotation anchors the ethical imperative: "If I am not for myself, then who will be? And if I am only for myself, then what am I? And if not now, when?" [V] [BT] (Ch 11.4).

The Bertha Jimenez / Rise Products case is offered as an example of mission-driven entrepreneurship using upcycled barley grain from craft breweries to make flour (Ch 11.4). TOMS Shoes is given as a "One for One" example. The chapter closes with a unifying statement: "The purpose of business is relationships, and the quality of relationships depends on our acceptance of self and concern for others. These are developed through the virtues of humility on the one hand and courage on the other." [V] (Ch 11.4).

## Part XII: Author's "succinct theory" (Appendix C)

Appendix C is a personal credo by the lead author. It states the most pointed positions in the book.

**Deontology over utilitarianism in business.** "Business ethics should be grounded in deontology more than in utilitarianism. That is, the ends should not typically be considered sufficient justification for the means when it comes to framing a business strategy. Rather, it is the means that ennoble the ends." [V] (App C, "The Nature of Business Ethics").

**Profits versus ethics is a false dichotomy.** "To pit ethics against profits and insist that a business leader must choose between the two is a false dichotomy. In truth, successful business can be practiced in ethical fashion." [V] (App C).

**Multiple stakeholders, all significant though not equal.** "An ethical business leader appreciates the existence of multiple stakeholders and accepts responsibility for all of them ... While not all stakeholders are equal in significance, they nonetheless all are significant." [V] (App C, "The Nature of the Ethical Business Leader").

**Pay disparity hurts teamwork.** "While successful executives are compensated more than their subordinates, it should not to be many multiples more. The less disparity that exists between the highest- and least-paid members of a company, the greater the level of teamwork and commitment that will prevail among all." [V] (App C).

**Stewardship, philanthropy, partnership.** Ethical entrepreneurs are "good stewards of the social and physical environments where they do business"; they "engage in both private and corporate philanthropy"; they treat themselves as "a partner or colleague or coworker, and so, a concerned boss" rather than as "a 'sir' or 'ma'am' to subordinates" [V] (App C).

**Restoring loyalty.** "One way to dispel [the management/labor divide] is for business leaders to take the first steps in restoring the broken sense of obligation that owners and employees owe each other. This may be the most important task of business leadership now and going forward." [V] (App C).

## Selected blockquotes (verbatim, character-verified against source)

> "business ethics ... the conduct by which companies and their agents abide by the law and respect the rights of their stakeholders, particularly their customers, clients, employees, and the surrounding community and environment."
>
> (Ch 1, "Key Terms"; restated Ch 1.1, "Acting with Integrity")

> "Considering only stockholders can often result in unethical decisions; the impact on all stakeholders must be considered and rationally assessed."
>
> (Ch 1.2, "Stockholders, Stakeholders, and Goodwill")

> "A deontologist would argue that you should adhere to particular duties in performing your actions, regardless of the parties with whom you interact. A utilitarian would say that any act you take should result in the greatest good for the greatest number. A virtue ethicist would state that you cannot be virtuous if you lack integrity in your behavior toward all."
>
> (Ch 1.3, "Multiple versus Single Ethical Standards")

> "Happiness is a kind of activity; and an activity clearly is developed and is not a piece of property already in one's possession."
>
> (Ch 2.1, "Aristotelian Virtue Ethics", citing Aristotle, *Nicomachean Ethics*)

> "magnanimity seems to be a sort of crown of the virtues, because it enhances them and is never found apart from them. This makes it hard to be truly magnanimous, because it is impossible without all-round excellence."
>
> (Ch 2.1, "Aristotelian Virtue Ethics", citing Aristotle)

> "the only purpose for which power can be rightfully exercised over any member of a civilized community, against his will, is to prevent harm to others. His own good, either physical or moral, is not a sufficient warrant."
>
> (Ch 2.4, "Synthesizing Rights and Utility", citing Mill, *On Liberty*)

> "Act only according to that maxim whereby you can, at the same time, will that it should become a universal law."
>
> (Ch 2.5, "Aroused From 'Dogmatic Slumber'", citing Kant, categorical imperative)

> "the interests of all stakeholders are of intrinsic value. That is, each group of stakeholders merits consideration for its own sake and not merely because of its ability to further the interests of some other group, such as the shareowners."
>
> (Ch 3.2, "The Ethical Basis of Stakeholders' Claims", citing Donaldson and Preston)

> "Diversity is being invited to the party; inclusion is being asked to dance. Diversity is about the ingredients, the mix of people and perspectives. Inclusion is about the container — the place that allows employees to feel they belong, to feel both accepted and different."
>
> (Ch 8.1, "Workplace Diversity", citing Howard J. Ross)

> "Business ethics should be grounded in deontology more than in utilitarianism. That is, the ends should not typically be considered sufficient justification for the means when it comes to framing a business strategy. Rather, it is the means that ennoble the ends."
>
> (App C, "The Nature of Business Ethics")

## Connections the author makes in the text

- **Aristotle** (virtue, eudaimonia, golden mean, phronesis): foundational, approving (Ch 1.1, Ch 2.1, App A) [BT].
- **Confucius** (li, junzi, dao of humanity, quan): foundational alongside Aristotle, approving (Ch 2.2-2.3) [BT].
- **Jeremy Bentham and John Stuart Mill** (utilitarianism, hedonic calculus, harm principle): foundational, presented with critique of consequentialism's blindness to character (Ch 2.4, App A) [BT].
- **Immanuel Kant** (deontology, categorical imperative): foundational, central to App C credo (Ch 2.5, App A, App C) [BT].
- **John Rawls** (justice as fairness, veil of ignorance, original position, difference principle): primary modern political-ethics source; Robert Nozick registered as critic (Ch 2.6, App A) [BT].
- **Milton Friedman** ("only social responsibility of business is to increase profits"): contrarian foil; explicitly framed against (Ch 1.2, Ch 4.1) [BT].
- **Henry Ford** and **Dodge v. Ford Motor Company (1919)**: shareholder-primacy precedent, treated as historical ground rather than current law (Ch 4.1, Ch 5.2) [BT].
- **Lynn Stout** (Cornell Law) and **Stephen Bainbridge** (UCLA Law): pro vs anti shareholder-primacy, NYT debate (Ch 3.1, Ch 4.1) [BT].
- **Donaldson and Preston**: stakeholder-theory taxonomy (descriptive, instrumental, normative); endorsed (Ch 3.2) [BT].
- **James Grunig and Todd Hunt**: organisational linkage model (enabling, normative, functional, diffused stakeholders); endorsed (Ch 3.2) [BT].
- **John Elkington**: triple bottom line (people, planet, profit); endorsed (Ch 3.4) [BT].
- **Garrett Hardin / William Forster Lloyd**: tragedy of the commons; approving (Ch 4.2) [BT].
- **Cormac Cullinan, Thomas Berry**: earth jurisprudence; sympathetic but factual (Ch 4.2) [BT].
- **A.C. Pigou**: Pigovian tax; neutral attribution (Ch 4.2) [BT].
- **Hofstede**: cross-cultural dimensions; not a primary section but referenced for cultural-difference framing (Ch 5).
- **Ida Tarbell** on John D. Rockefeller: critical, "scruples in an electric dynamo" quoted (Ch 5.2) [BT].
- **Charles Dickens, Upton Sinclair, Rachel Carson, Betty Friedan, Kate Chopin, Chinua Achebe**: socially-critical literary witnesses to industrial harms; approvingly cited (Ch 3.4, Ch 5.2) [BT].
- **Peter Drucker** ("workers need to know the organization's mission and to believe in it"); "Culture eats strategy for breakfast" alluded to in Ch 11; approving (Ch 4.1, Ch 11) [BT].
- **Max Weber** on bureaucracy as advanced-capitalism feature: approvingly cited; quoted directly (Ch 9.1) [BT].
- **John Kenneth Galbraith** (*The Affluent Society*, dependence effect): critical of advertising-driven demand; cited approvingly (Ch 9.2) [BT].
- **Vance Packard** (*The Hidden Persuaders*) and **James Vicary**: subliminal-advertising claim treated as inconclusive (Ch 9.2) [BT].
- **The American Medical Association** (1920 opposition to government insurance): historical attribution, neutral (Ch 9.4) [BT].
- **Samuel Lloyd / Lloyd's of London**: insurance origin; neutral attribution (Ch 9.3) [BT].
- **Robert Reich** (former US Labor Secretary): cited for income-inequality observations and gig-economy critique; approving (Ch 8.4, Ch 10.3) [BT].
- **Lloyd Blankfein** (Goldman Sachs CEO): cited approvingly on income inequality (Ch 8.4) [BT].
- **Bill Gross** (PIMCO): cited approvingly on minimum-wage increase (Ch 8.4) [BT].
- **David Neumark and William L. Wascher**: counter-position on minimum-wage poverty effects; registered without endorsement (Ch 8.4) [BT].
- **Christopher D. Stone** ("Should Trees Have Standing?", 1972): legal-standing-for-environment proposal; approvingly cited (Ch 3.2) [BT].
- **Stephen Latham** (Yale Hastings Program): animal-testing regulatory continuum; approvingly cited (Ch 8.5) [BT].
- **Howard J. Ross** (Cook Ross): "diversity is invitation, inclusion is dance" quote; approvingly cited (Ch 8.1) [BT].
- **McKinsey & Company**: diversity dividend, automation impact, sustainability surveys; approvingly cited (Ch 4.2, Ch 8.1, Ch 10.4) [BT].
- **Mike Dillon** (PwC) and **David Rock** (Neuro-Leadership Institute): diversity advocates; approvingly cited (Ch 8.1) [BT].
- **Richard DeGeorge** (Univ. of Kansas): humanities-based business ethics; approvingly cited (Ch 5.4) [BT].
- **Lee Jae-yong** (Samsung), **FIFA**, **Volkswagen**, **LIBOR** (Barclay's, JPMorgan Chase, Citicorp, RBS, Deutsche Bank), **Theranos**, **Equifax**, **Petrobras**, **Operation Car Wash**: scandal exemplars (Ch 5.4, Ch 11) [BT].

## Positions the author explicitly frames against

- **Shareholder primacy as exclusive duty** (Friedman, Bainbridge, *Dodge v. Ford*): rejected in favour of stakeholder pluralism (Ch 1.2, Ch 4.1) [AR].
- **Ethical relativism** ("when in Rome"): rejected in favour of a single ethical standard applicable across contexts (Ch 1.3) [AR].
- **Compliance-as-checkbox ethics training**: rejected in favour of culture and conscience-formation (Ch 5.4) [AR].
- **Pure utilitarian "ends justify means" reasoning in business**: rejected; deontology preferred (App C) [AR].
- **Pay secrecy** (states without bans): rejected; transparency favoured (Ch 7.5) [AR].
- **Inflexible "no-fault" attendance policies that disregard ADA accommodations** (Verizon): rejected (Ch 8.2) [AR].
- **Excessive non-compete agreements covering low-wage workers** (Jimmy John's): rejected; California's 2017 law is approved (Ch 7.1) [AR].
- **Greenwashing** (superficial CSR claims): rejected (Ch 3.4) [AR].
- **Use of contract workers to dodge benefits and payroll taxes** (Microsoft pre-settlement): rejected (Ch 10.3) [AR].
- **Unpaid internships used as labour**: rejected; pay or academic credit required (Ch 10.3) [AR].
- **Workplace harassment, even when long-tenured patterns**: rejected; the Ford repeat-offender pattern is treated as ethical and legal failure (Ch 6.1) [AR].
- **Bribery as cultural accommodation in international business**: rejected; the FCPA position is endorsed (Ch 5, Ch 7.4) [AR].
- **Insurance redlining by neighbourhood demographics**: rejected (Ch 9.3) [AR].
- **CEO pay ratios of ~300×**: presented as evidence of capitalism's reward distribution distortion (Ch 6.3, App C) [AR].

## Citation and source-integrity notes

- **Conversion path:** PDF → markdown via pymupdf4llm 0.2.9. Page anchors are not preserved; citation style is `(Ch N, "Section name")` for chapters and `(App {A|B|C}, "Section name")` for appendices. Some section headings in the converted markdown carry markdown emphasis artefacts (e.g., `### **2.1 The Concept of Ethical Business in Ancient Athens**`); the names used in citations are the readable section titles without emphasis markers.
- **Coverage:** Option B (substantive content only). Per-chapter scaffolding sections excluded: **Key Terms**, **Summary**, **Assessment Questions**, **Endnotes**. Back-matter excluded: Answer Key (lines 19388-19854) and Index (lines 19853-20239). Appendices A, B, C are included as substantive framework / case content. Per the source-integrity rule, this partial-coverage scope is declared explicitly here; all substantive prose is included.
- **Verbatim quotations** were verified character-by-character against the source markdown in Pass D. Where the source uses American English spelling, the verbatim quote preserves it; surrounding paraphrase uses British English per the corpus convention. OpenStax's typographical conventions (em dashes, smart quotes) are preserved in blockquotes.
- **Evidence-class markers** ([V] verbatim; [AP] author paraphrase; [AR] author argument; [AE] author example; [BT] borrowed-through, the source attributes to a third party) are inline with claims throughout.
- **Image classification** for Pass H: per the parallel-batch instructions, JPEG is the dominant format (95 of 103 files, ~92%) versus PNG (8 files). Front-matter and chapter-opener photos are decorative; figure-style PNGs that depict frameworks (stakeholder linkage, triple bottom line, pay/income charts, gender-pay-gap graphics, codetermination tables) are substantive. The substantive count for this book is comparatively low — most "figures" in *Business Ethics* are stock photos illustrating the surrounding case rather than concept diagrams.
- **No task-application guidance** is included in this deep ref. Diagnostic questions, anti-patterns, and worked examples are projected into the distillations at `corpus.commons/demo/distillations/{decision-making,stakeholder-engagement}/openstax-business-ethics-{task}.md`.
- **No training-data leakage**. All claims trace to passages in the source markdown. Where the source attributes a position to a third party (Aristotle, Confucius, Bentham, Mill, Kant, Rawls, Donaldson and Preston, Friedman, Hardin, Galbraith, Weber, Drucker, Reich, Blankfein, etc.), the marker [BT] travels with the citation.
